scenarioName: Unstable SVT - Failed Vagal and Adenosine, Synchronized Cardioversion

scenarioStory.history: 48-year-old female was sitting at her home office desk when she felt her heart suddenly start racing approximately 30 minutes ago. She describes a 'fluttering, pounding' sensation in her chest. Within a few minutes she became lightheaded, weak, and developed central chest pressure rated 5/10. Her husband noticed she looked pale and was struggling to put words together coherently. She has had two prior similar episodes over the past 2 years - one resolved spontaneously after breath-holding, and one required an ED visit where she was reportedly told she had 'a fast rhythm called SVT' and discharged with a follow-up plan she has not yet kept. No prior cardioversion or ablation. No new medications, no caffeine binge today, no decongestants, no recreational drugs, no recent illness. Denies pregnancy (verified - currently menstruating).
scenarioStory.discussion: Differentiate stable from unstable tachycardia using the unstable criteria: hypotension, altered mental status, chest pain, signs of shock, acute heart failure. Apply the SVT algorithm: vagal -> adenosine 6 mg -> adenosine 12 mg -> if stable, calcium channel blocker or beta-blocker; if unstable, synchronized cardioversion. Use the modified Valsalva maneuver (15 sec forced expiration against syringe + supine with passive leg raise) for higher conversion rates. Administer adenosine via the most proximal IV possible with rapid 20 mL flush due to its 10-second half-life. Verify SYNCHRONIZED mode before every cardioversion and re-engage SYNC after each shock if escalating. Use 100 J biphasic as the typical first-shock energy for unstable SVT (escalate to 150, 200 J as needed). Provide procedural sedation (midazolam) and analgesia (fentanyl) for any conscious patient receiving synchronized cardioversion. Identify post-conversion ECG findings: pre-excitation (delta waves) implies WPW and changes future therapy. Recognize iatrogenic VF from asynchronous shock as a preventable error of missed SYNC verification
scenarioStory.course: Initial Presentation - Borderline Unstable SVT: Crew arrives to a 48-year-old female with rapid narrow-complex tachycardia at 210, hypotension (SBP 86), and early hemodynamic compromise (lightheadedness, central chest pressure, mild altered mental status, diaphoresis). Patient meets criteria for 'unstable' tachycardia per the protocol (chest pain, hemodynamic compromise). However, she is still conversant and the team has time for one focused vagal maneuver attempt and a single adenosine push while preparing synchronized cardioversion in parallel. → Vagal and Adenosine Failed - Patient Decompensating: Vagal maneuver produced a brief change on the monitor but no conversion. Adenosine 6 mg IV with rapid flush produced a few seconds of asystole on the monitor (expected), then the SVT immediately resumed at the same rate. The crew gave a second dose of Adenosine 12 mg IV - again no sustained conversion. Patient blood pressure has now dropped further (SBP 78) and her mental status is worsening. She is clearly unstable and requires immediate synchronized cardioversion. The pads are already on, sedation/analgesia drawn up, and the device is in SYNC mode at 100 J. → Conversion to Sinus Rhythm - Stabilization and Transport: Synchronized cardioversion at 100 J converted the SVT to sinus rhythm at 92 bpm. Blood pressure recovers to SBP 118, mental status clears within 1-2 minutes, and the patient reports the chest pressure has resolved. The crew reassesses, leaves pads in place, acquires a post-conversion 12-lead, transports to the closest hospital with cardiac/EP capability, and provides early notification.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden onset of rapid heart rate, palpitations, lightheadedness, chest pressure, and feeling like she might pass out

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Presentation - Borderline Unstable SVT
scenarioEvents.description: Crew arrives to a 48-year-old female with rapid narrow-complex tachycardia at 210, hypotension (SBP 86), and early hemodynamic compromise (lightheadedness, central chest pressure, mild altered mental status, diaphoresis). Patient meets criteria for 'unstable' tachycardia per the protocol (chest pain, hemodynamic compromise). However, she is still conversant and the team has time for one focused vagal maneuver attempt and a single adenosine push while preparing synchronized cardioversion in parallel.

scenarioEvents.name: Vagal and Adenosine Failed - Patient Decompensating
scenarioEvents.description: Vagal maneuver produced a brief change on the monitor but no conversion. Adenosine 6 mg IV with rapid flush produced a few seconds of asystole on the monitor (expected), then the SVT immediately resumed at the same rate. The crew gave a second dose of Adenosine 12 mg IV - again no sustained conversion. Patient blood pressure has now dropped further (SBP 78) and her mental status is worsening. She is clearly unstable and requires immediate synchronized cardioversion. The pads are already on, sedation/analgesia drawn up, and the device is in SYNC mode at 100 J.

scenarioEvents.name: Conversion to Sinus Rhythm - Stabilization and Transport
scenarioEvents.description: Synchronized cardioversion at 100 J converted the SVT to sinus rhythm at 92 bpm. Blood pressure recovers to SBP 118, mental status clears within 1-2 minutes, and the patient reports the chest pressure has resolved. The crew reassesses, leaves pads in place, acquires a post-conversion 12-lead, transports to the closest hospital with cardiac/EP capability, and provides early notification.

scenarioEvents.name: Branch - Treated as Stable SVT, Continued with Diltiazem
scenarioEvents.description: After adenosine failed, the crew elected to treat the patient as stable SVT and gave diltiazem. The patient was not stable - she had hypotension, chest pain, and altered mental status. Diltiazem in a hypotensive SVT patient further drops the blood pressure precipitously due to its negative inotropic and vasodilatory effects. The patient is now severely hypotensive and obtunded, approaching peri-arrest. The crew must recognize the error and proceed to immediate synchronized cardioversion with vasopressor support.

scenarioEvents.name: Branch - Asynchronous Shock Induced VF
scenarioEvents.description: The defibrillator was inadvertently left in defib mode (not SYNC) when the shock was delivered. The shock landed on the T wave (relative refractory period) and induced ventricular fibrillation. The patient is now in cardiac arrest. The crew must immediately recognize the new rhythm, initiate compressions, and defibrillate (now appropriately in defib mode at maximum energy 200 J) following the cardiac arrest algorithm.

checklist.title: Recognize regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 210 with hemodynamic compromise (hypotension, AMS, chest pain) as unstable SVT
Establish proximal large-bore IV (antecubital) for adenosine delivery
Acquire 12-lead ECG to confirm SVT (rule out wide-complex tachycardia)
Perform a vagal maneuver (modified Valsalva preferred)
Administer Adenosine 6 mg IV with rapid 20 mL NS flush; if no conversion, repeat with Adenosine 12 mg IV with rapid flush
Recognize when adenosine fails in an unstable patient: proceed to synchronized cardioversion, NOT diltiazem or metoprolol
Provide procedural sedation (Midazolam 2 mg IV) and analgesia (Fentanyl 50 mcg IV) before synchronized cardioversion when patient is conscious
Verify SYNCHRONIZED mode is active (look for marker over each R wave) before pressing shock
Deliver synchronized cardioversion at 100 J biphasic; press and HOLD the shock button until the device delivers on the next R wave
After conversion: leave pads on, acquire post-conversion 12-lead, monitor for recurrence
Transport to a hospital with cardiology/EP capability and provide early notification
Position the patient supine to support preload and facilitate Valsalva
Confirm there is no pre-excitation (delta waves) on the post-conversion 12-lead, as it changes future medication selection
If SBP remains < 100 after conversion, give a 250-500 mL NS bolus
Document time of onset of SVT, duration, vagal/adenosine attempts, sedation/analgesia given, energy and time of cardioversion, time of conversion to sinus
